title: An anti Christianity interpretation of Eva
display_title: An anti Christianity interpretation of Eva
wikitext:
[]: An anti-Christianity interpretation of Eva Post by Born of Lilith » Sat Sep 25, 2010 7:55 pm Alright, I know that most of yall are already facepalming, so before I type out my proposal I'll give yall a bit of insight on my perspective. I am a Christian and it was my Christianity that led me to become interested in Eva (z0mg a xian amine bout a lion preachin teh jeebus? awsum). Likewise, I've searched high and low for some trace of a Christian allegory in the series and I've found that Eva's religious subtext is more in tune with Gnosticism than Christianity (I haven't studied Gnosticism much myself, but the shoe seems to fit). I can accept that, but what relevance does that leave the Christian references with? Well, it came to me today: what if the purpose of them is to skew the Christian understanding of symbols and images that are almost exclusively tied to it nowadays, leading to a less biased/partial perception of them? To address the two otherwise inevitable retorts in advance: Yes, I'm reading into things and yes, I know that the assistant director said the symbolism is meaningless. Being a story that utilizes more subtext than concrete answers, I don't think it's possible to closely examine Eva without "reading into it" at least a little bit. Also, I'm sure Kazuya Tsurumaki doesn't know exactly what was going through Anno's head when he concocted the series' heady plot, so I think scrutiny of the religious overtones is still relevant. First off, there's the "crossplosions" and other use of the symbol most commonly associated with Jesus Christ. Could the point be to distance us from that association so that we think of it solely as a symbol of sacrifice? Then the Angels. I know that they aren't supposed to be the same angels that watch over you at night, but could the purpose of their "nickname" be to instill a more morally ambiguous perception of angels in the viewer? And finally, God. If you receive Eva's plot through a Christian lens, then certainly SEELE and Gendo Ikari are giving God the middle finger through their secular humanist, technology-over-natural order schemes, and as such, they're "bad guys". But if we look past that preconceived notion, it seems entirely plausible that Instrumentality is union with God. Or, if Instrumentality is overcoming God, then (because NERV are presented as the protagonists) we can conclude that defying God is a good choice in the Eva universe. Either way, this ain't the big guy upstairs as your daddy knows him. I recognize that I've taken lots of fanwank license with my interpretation, but I thought it was all too "coincidental" to dismiss as a passing muse. Further insight/counterarguments welcome!

[]: Post by Sanada Yukimura » Sat Sep 25, 2010 8:10 pm hello, I'm not christian though,but i know a thing and two about it. Well,i read it but i didn't understand so much the essence of your theory.My english is a lot bad i know ,i could not realized the core of your ideas. I just think "Angels" were named like that because of Manuscript of Dead Sea(or somewhere around Arabia).Maybe they are the angels of Apocalypse....I didn't pay so much attention in the plot itself. And for me Gendo and SEELE was just ignoring God with their "powers"(technology,etc) but in the end they want to fill the emptiness of themselves,which means they want in some way a "union with God"something like "I and God as one" because in christian point of view "God is the all and everything,completed in Himself"

[]: Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sat Sep 25, 2010 8:19 pm I'm not sure if the series is intentionally "anti-Christian". Christianity in Japan isn't a common religion, so it's either seen as kind of creepy (almost in the same way Western entertainment viewed Eastern religions some time ago), or just used to "look cool". ( Nadia - The Secret Of Blue Water , for example.) That being said, I do agree that, whereas it may not be intentional, there does seem to be an "anti-Christian" atmosphere about Evangelion. Just look at how the Marduk Institute seems to "appoint" those that are "chosen", only for it to be revealed that the Marduk Institute doesn't exist, and used only to manipulate other men. Especially considering the origins of the name Marduk , it does seem to be a commentary about the idea of a supernatural being of absolute power. Though it could also be a commentary on organized Western religions in general. Though, I think that in the film The End of Evangelion when all of humanity is joined into one being, we see how much that doesn't actually work. (The joining of all of humanity is a very Humanistic idea that results in us being our own "gods".) So whereas the heads of NERV and SEELE are definitely anti-Christian, their Humanistic plans of operating within the "powers" of joined humanity ultimately blow up in their face and humanity is left to try and figure it all out again. So, I'm pretty sure the creators of NGE don't know actually know what they think about God and Christianity. And I think it shows in their works. P.S. I am also a Christian. It's cool to see someone else also attempt to deconstruct the Christian images within the show.

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Sep 25, 2010 9:44 pm As for my (scattered) interpretations on the significance of the Biblical symbolism, see See here , here , here , and here. . To more directly answer your question, I don't think NGE is anti-Christian as much as it is apathetic towards Christianity, and all religions, really, because I don't see NGE as a series that's really concerned with religion. Its appropriating of religious concepts seems to mostly to feed into the sense of portentousness, drama, and, more obliquely, into the archetypal themes being expressed. Born of Lilith wrote: what if the purpose of them is to skew the Christian understanding of symbols and images that are almost exclusively tied to it nowadays, leading to a less biased/partial perception of them?

[]: Post by LeoXiao » Sun Sep 26, 2010 10:35 am I don't think NGE is anti-Christian as much as it is antipathetic towards Christianity, and all religions, really, because I don't see NGE as a series that's really concerned with religion. Its appropriating of religious concepts seems to mostly to feed into the sense of portentousness, drama, and, more obliquely, into the archetypal themes being expressed.

[]: Post by Allemann » Sun Sep 26, 2010 12:59 pm It's a mistake to label Evangelion as antichristian, because the show is neutral on the topic of religion, and all the references serve for the purpose of creating an atmosphere. No one should have his religious feelings offended by it. It's harmless fun. Furthermore, NGE takes more references from Jewish folklore and medieval mysticism then orthodox Christianity or orthodox Judaism. No reason to panic. I interpret Evangelion as anti-everything, since I understand its message as nihilistic.

[]: Post by NAveryW » Sun Sep 26, 2010 12:59 pm Kazuya Tsurumaki wrote: There are a lot of giant robot shows in Japan, and we did want our story to have a religious theme to help distinguish us. Because Christianity is an uncommon religion in Japan we thought it would be mysterious. None of the staff who worked on Eva are Christians. There is no actual Christian meaning to the show, we just thought the visual symbols of Christianity look cool. If we had known the show would get distributed in the US and Europe we might have rethought that choice.

[]: Re: An anti-Christianity interpretation of Eva Post by Hyper Shinchan » Sun Sep 26, 2010 9:41 pm Born of Lilith wrote: Also, I'm sure Kazuya Tsurumaki doesn't know exactly what was going through Anno's head when he concocted the series' heady plot, so I think scrutiny of the religious overtones is still relevant.

[]: Re: An anti-Christianity interpretation of Eva Post by Azathoth » Sun Sep 26, 2010 10:07 pm Hyper Shinchan wrote: If you read the Bible they're already quite morally ambiguos: "That night the angel of the Lord went forth and struck down one hundred and eighty five thousand men in the Assyrian camp. Early the next morning, there they were, all the corpuses of the dead. (2 Kings 19:35 NAB)" and there are many other examples in that weird book, go ahead and keep reading it, it could be interesting or you could save some time and check this website

[]: Re: An anti-Christianity interpretation of Eva Post by Hyper Shinchan » Sun Sep 26, 2010 11:28 pm Azathoth wrote: That's not morally ambiguous inasmuch as the people in question are Babylonians. Or Assyrians, or something; the point is that they aren't Jews, and therefore in the context of the Bible were not worth worrying about. It is not the only instance of God-approved genocide (or God-performed genocide) in the Bible, and the intended audience was not supposed to conclude that God or angels were evil because of it!

It's the apotheosis of the intolerance. People should just forget about religions, they're the cause of many problems in our modern society just like it was in past societies.
